I have nothing to disclose. For right-sided colon cancer, Laparoscopic CME is technically challenging because of the anatomical complexity of its region. To overcome the difficulty of its surgery, we focused on the embryology and anatomy of the transverse mesocolon. The usefulness of a cranial approach was also elucidated from the embryological point of view.

So we lastly, development of gastrointestinal tract with figures. This is a lateral view of the primary gastrointestinal tract at four to five weeks of embryonic development. The midgut grows in length and forms a primary intestinal loop. And this blue area corresponds to the transverse mesocolon. Until the third month, the intestinal loop rotates to a hundred seventy degree counterclockwise around SMA and SMV. Until the fifth month, the transverse mesocolon covers the frontal surfaces of the pancreas and duodenum like this. At this time, superior right colic vein sometimes joins the right gastroepipolar vein and gastrocolic trunk is formed. So this anatomical complexity of the transverse mesocolon due to torsion and fusion needs to be simplified.

The surgeon uses a cranial approach to dissect the transverse mesocolon from the pancreatic head and duodenum by entering an avascular plane. The superior right colic vein is divided at its origin. The folded mesocolon of the right two-thirds of the transverse colon is spread out and mobilized. The middle colic vein is then divided at its origin, followed by lymph node dissection along the superior mesenteric border in a cranial to caudal direction.

The surgeon opens the omental bursa widely and dissects the transverse mesocolon from the pancreas head and duodenum by entering the embryological avascular plane. The superior right colic vein is divided, exposing the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and artery (SMA). The medial colic vein and artery are divided, and lymph node dissection along the SMV is performed from cranial to caudal. The transverse colon is raised ventrally, and the mesocolon is separated from the lateral peritoneal tissue using a medial to lateral approach. Adhesions between the transverse mesocolon and the stomach or the pedicle of the right gastroepiploic vessels are dissected sharply. The right gastroepiploic vein is identified, and the boundaries of the right gastroepiploic vessels, greater omentum, and transverse mesocolon are delineated. The greater omentum is dissected from the pancreatic head.

The surgeon isolates and divides the superior right colic vein at its origin, noting the presence of two SRCVs in this patient. The transverse mesocolon is fully mobilized, exposing the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and medial colic vein, which is divided at its origin. The duodenum and pancreas are visible. The superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and middle colic artery are divided, followed by the division of the ileocolic artery and vein. Lymph node dissection continues as the transverse colon is raised ventrally, and the mesocolon, including its pedicles, is separated from the lateral peritoneal tissues using a medial to lateral approach. The duodenum is visible in the surgical field.

A gauze is inserted into the dissected space, serving as a landmark for the dissection of the lateral attachment of the ascending colon. The mobilization of the right colon is completed, revealing the operator's view with visible structures such as the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), right gastroepiploic vein, and gastrocolic trunk. An extracorporeal anastomosis is performed.
